text: The surgical team positions themselves on the patient's left side, with the patient in a 30-degree Trendelenburg position and the table tilted 15 degrees to the left. Multiple trocars are used for access. The region is retracted anterior medially to identify the ileocolic pedicle. The peritoneum over the pedicle is scored, and the pedicle is isolated using an ultrasonically activated scalpel. The iliocolic artery and vein are dissected and transected at their origins between hemlock clips. Mesenteric dissection is extended up to the root of the middle colic vessels, which are clipped and transected. Dissection continues toward the hepatic flexure, which is freed using the ultrasonic scalpel. The medial dissection is completed, and the attachments of the appendix and lateral attachments of the right colon are freed. The terminal ileum is prepared and transected using an endoscopic linear stapler.

The surgeon applies caudal traction on the transverse colon to enter the bursa mentalis, dividing the gastrocolic tissue towards the splenic flexure using an ultrasonic scalpel. The surgical team then changes position to the right side of the patient for dissection of the left colon and rectum. The patient is repositioned in a thirty-degree Trendelenburg position with the operating table tilted fifteen degrees to the right. Small bowel loops are retracted into the right upper quadrant, and lateral retraction of the sigmoid colon allows incision of the peritoneum at the promontorium level. The dissection continues in the aorta mesenteric window up to the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, with care taken to preserve the left ureter, adult vessels, and autonomic nerves.

The surgeon clips the inferior mesenteric artery with hemlock clips, revealing the autonomic nerve plexus. After transecting the artery, the left mesocolon is dissected using a medial to lateral approach. The inferior mesenteric vein is similarly clipped and divided. The left colon is retracted medially, and its lateral attachments are divided, allowing the splenic flexure to be taken down.

The surgeon directs attention to the pelvis, freeing the posterior side of the mesorectum by incising the lateral peritoneal reflection below the sacral promontory and mobilizing it from the sacral hollow. The hypogastric nerves are visible on the posterior side. The rectum is dissected according to total mesorectal excision principles, preserving the fascia propria recti through sharp dissection between the fascia propria recti and the pre-sacral fascia. After posterior dissection, lateral and anterior mesorectal dissections follow, with the right and left sides of the mesorectum mobilized. The dissection continues circumferentially down to the pelvic floor until the anorectal ring is reached, preparing the distal rectum for transection.

The surgeon performs transection of the rectum using an articulating endoscopic linear stapler with a green cartridge, leaving the rectal stump visible between the levator ani muscles. The suprapubic trocar site is extended into a suprapubic incision, through which the specimen is extracted. A J-pouch is constructed using a stapler.

The surgeon performs a water leak test on the newly constructed J-pouch. The anvil of the circular stapler is placed inside the J-pouch, and the circular stapler is introduced trans-anally to create a pouch-anal anastomosis, ensuring the small intestine is not twisted during the procedure. A diverting loop ileostomy is constructed at a pre-marked site proximal to the pouch. The video concludes with a view of the post-operative site.
